description: Figure 13 presents a flow chart of typical thermomechanical processing routes for producing lightweight Fe-Al-(C), Fe-Al-Mn-C, and Fe-Mn-Al-C ferritic and multiphase sheet steels at laboratory scale. The left branch shows the hot-rolling sequence: ingot casting, reheating at 1250 °C, rough rolling, a second reheating at 1250 °C, hot rolling to a final thickness of 3.5 mm with a finishing rolling temperature (FRT) of 900 °C, followed by run-out table cooling and coiling. The right branch depicts the downstream cold/warm processing chain: pickling at 85 °C in HCl, cold rolling or warm rolling at 250 °C to a final thickness of 1 mm, continuous annealing at 850–900 °C, and final aging at 400–700 °C. The flow chart explicitly labels Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C alloys for the hot-rolling path and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys for the cold-rolling and annealing path. The accompanying text explains that processing parameters depend on alloy chemistry, with high cooling rates after hot rolling recommended to suppress brittle j-carbide precipitation during coiling, and that coiling temperatures vary by alloy type (e.g., ~650 °C for ultra-low-C Fe-Al-(C) versus ~400 °C or room temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C). It also notes that warm rolling at 250 °C is required for ferritic alloys containing more than 8 wt.% Al to avoid cracking, and that cold reductions up to 66.67 % can be applied to multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.
